content.full_text:
Law Offices of

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH

July 31, 1990

HAND DELIVERED

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

RE: YOUR LETTER REGARDING LETTER NO. 90-492

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am in receipt of your letter dated July 30,
1990, with regard to my recent letter to you (dated
July 23). Although your letter appears to be a form
letter in part, since it makes specific reference to my
conversation with John Wallace on July 26, I want to
make sure the record is clear.

I provided you with a series of "real life hypo-
thetical questions" for consideration by the staff in
its memorandum to the Commissioners regarding advice
the staff is giving on certain questions discussed at
last month's Commission meeting. It was my understand-
ing from the discussion at the "pre-agenda" meeting on
July 23 that such questions were appropriate for sub-
mission to the staff for discussion at the next Commission meeting.

On July 26, John Wallace phoned me to say that the
staff did not intend to address my questions in the
memorandum for the upcoming Commission meeting. The
staff intended to focus the memorandum in another area.
Instead the staff wanted to treat my letter as one for
"informal assistance." Mr. Wallace was seeking my
concurrence in that transformation. I explained to Mr.
Wallace that I was reluctant to agree, since an "informal assistance" response might be months in arriving.
The questions posed in my letter are ones which must be
dealt with before the November election cycle begins.

Mr. Wallace gave me his personal assurance that my
letter would be responded to within 21 working days, as
if it were a "formal advice" letter. He pointed out
that it would have an "I" number rather than an "A"
number. With that assurance, I agreed to my letter
being treated as one seeking "informal assistance."
Therefore, it would not need to be addressed in,

LANCE H. OLSON
BRUCE J. HAGEL
LEROY Y. FONG
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH

GEORGE M. WATERS
DIANE M. FISHBURN

OF COUNSEL
LLOYD C. CONNELLY, Member
California State Legislature

300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, California 95814
TELEPHONE: (916) 442-2952
FAX: (916) 442-1280

Scott Hallabrin
July 31, 1990
Page Two


or attached to, the staff memorandum to the Commissioners for the
August meeting.

Consequently, it is my express understanding that my July
23, 1990 letter will be responded to within 21 working days
(which I calculate to be on or before August 21). If that is not
an accurate belief on my part, please advise at once.

Very truly yours,

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH

ROBERT E. LEIDIGH

/ccz

cc: John Wallace

Law Offices of

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH


July 23, 1990


Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814
                                                                HAND DELIVERED

RE:  QUESTIONS FOR STAFF'S CONSIDERATION REGARDING
     "PERSONAL USE" AND "TRUST" PROVISIONS OF THE ACT

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     At the last Commission meeting, interested persons
were invited to provide hypothetical questions to the
staff.  These would be considered by staff in preparing
a memorandum for the Commission's next meeting.  The
purpose of the memorandum is to review staff answers to
advice questions on the subject of the "personal use"
and "trust" provisions of the Act.

     I submit the following "real life" hypothetical
questions for the staff's consideration and responses.
These questions relate to items (1), (3) and (6) on
pages 8-9 of the last staff memorandum.

     (1)  Ballot measure committees:  Staff's
memorandum advises that a candidate may contribute to a
ballot measure committee "if the measure concerns an
issue which the candidate has made a part of his or her
campaign."

          (a)  A local elected official has three more
years to run in her current term.  There is a statewide
ballot measure on the issue of abortion.  The local
official wishes to contribute from campaign funds to
the ballot measure in order to demonstrate her support
for the issue.  She knows many of her constituents feel
the issue is very important.  However, she had
no opposition in her last re-election campaign and
likely will have no opposition in her next campaign.
Consequently, it is impossible to tell at this time
whether the abortion question will be an issue in her
next campaign three years hence.  May she contribute
with campaign funds?

          (b)  A local elected official has three more
years to run on his current term.  Rent control is an
important issue in his community.  In another local

LANCE H. OLSON
BRUCE J. HAGEL
LEROY K. FONG
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH
GEORGE M. WATERS
DIANE M. FISHBURN

OF COUNSEL
LLOYD G. CONNELLY, Member
California State Legislature


300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, California 95814
TELEPHONE: (916) 442-2952
FAX: (916) 442-1280

06.11 62 I1    E2 IMP

3 ddJ1

Scott Hallabrin
July 23, 1990
Page 2

jurisdiction (100 miles away) there is a ballot measure dealing
with rent control. He wishes to contribute to the ballot measure
in order to show his support for the issue. There is a case
currently pending before the United States Supreme Court which
may resolve all rent control issues on constitutional grounds.
The case will be decided after the ballot measure is voted on,
but before the local elected official's next campaign. Depending
on the result of the Supreme Court decision, the rent control
issue may be moot long before the local elected official's next
re-election campaign. May he contribute to the ballot measure
with his campaign funds?

(c) A local elected official believes strongly that
smoking and its side effects (including "second-hand" smoke) are
detrimental to humans' health. She has been in the forefront of
efforts at the local level to adopt and implement no smoking
ordinances governing public buildings of her local jurisdiction.
She is in the first year of a four-year term. Tobacco industry
lobbyists are actively lobbying the state legislature to adopt
state-wide standards for regulating smoking in public (and
private buildings). The antismoking forces are considering
circulating a statewide initiative to counter such legislation.
They have solicited her for a contribution. In her recent
election campaign, growth-control, gang violence and sports
stadium were the major campaign issues. The smoking ordinance
was not. However, there is strong public support in her
community for the ordinance. May she contribute to the statewide
ballot measure campaign from her campaign funds?

(d) A state legislator represents a district which
includes a jurisdiction with a strong rent control ordinance.
His next election will be in two years. Another jurisdiction
which has a similar strong rent control ordinance has a ballot
measure to be voted on this year which would significantly weaken
that jurisdiction's rent control ordinance. Tenant activists in
the legislator's district fear that if the ballot measure
succeeds, it will increase the likelihood of a similar measure
being proposed in their jurisdiction. They urge the legislator
to contribute to the campaign against the ballot measure. May he
do so with his campaign funds?

(e) Suppose instead of a ballot measure, there is a
lawsuit which has been brought challenging the constitutionality
of the rent control measure. The defenders of the other
jurisdiction's rent control measure are raising funds to provide
for the legal defense of the measure. May the legislator

Scott Hallabrin
July 23, 1990
Page 3


contribute to the legal defense fund from campaign funds? Would
the answer be different in either of the above situations if the
ballot measure or lawsuit involved the rent control ordinance in
the legislator's own district?

(3) Use of campaign funds to contribute to a federal
California candidate. Staff's memorandum advises that generally,
such contributions are not permissible, absent a showing that the
expenditure is associated with the candidate's office or
election.

(a) A local candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser
of a local candidate for federal office. The purpose of
attending is to demonstrate solidarity with the federal candidate
(and thereby acquire some party identification for a "non-
partisan" office). May the local candidate use campaign funds to
purchase a ticket to the fundraiser?

(b) A local candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser
of a candidate for federal office from a different locale. The
purpose of attending is to mingle with contributors to the
federal candidate who might be induced to contribute to the local
candidate, since the two share similar views on many issues. May
the local candidate use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to
the fundraiser?

(c) A local candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser
of a local candidate for federal office. The purpose of
attending is to show that the local office candidate has the
ability to "get the ear" of the federal candidate for federal office.
This would demonstrate the local candidate's ability to seek
federal assistance for issues of local concern. May the local
candidate use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to the
fundraiser?

(d) A legislative candidate wishes to attend a
fundraiser for her party's candidate for President. The
candidate is from Texas, not California. May she use campaign
funds to purchase a ticket to the fundraiser?

(6) Contributions to out-of-state political committees.
Staff's memorandum advises the general rule is that a candidate
may not give campaign funds to out-of-state committees.

(a) A local candidate has two years to run on her
term. She may or may not decide to seek re-election; however,
she has filed a Form 501 for re-election. She, and many of her
constituents, are committed on the abortion issue. A ballot
measure is pending in Arizona on the abortion question. It is

Scott Hallabrin
July 23, 1990
Page 4

expected to make the test case which the United States Supreme
Court will use to determine the continuing applicability of Roe
v. Wade. May she use campaign funds to contribute to the ballot
measure committee in Arizona?

(b) A local candidate is running for election in a
district whose voters are predominantly Black. Andrew Young is
in a run-off for the nomination for Governor of Georgia. May the
local candidate use campaign funds to contribute to Andrew Young?

(c) Suppose the candidate in question (b) above is a
candidate for state legislative office in a district whose voters
are predominantly Black. Would the answer be any different?

(d) Suppose the candidate in question (c) above is
running in a district with no significant Black population.
Would the answer be any different?

(e) A candidate for local office is a woman. She is
receiving campaign funding support from the National Women's
Political Caucus. She has been asked to, in turn, supply
campaign funding support for other women candidates in other
states. May she use her campaign funds for this purpose?

As I understand it, the Commission has asked the staff to
just provide Yes or No answers to the questions, rather than go
into great detail as to the reasoning. In that spirit, I have
not asked for the reasons why the answers might differ between
various questions posed herein. I hope that these questions are
helpful to the staff in indicating its interpretation. I look
forward to reading the answers.

Very truly yours,

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH

[signature]
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH

/ccz

3 d d 3

Although your letter appears to be a form letter in part, since it makes specific reference to my conversation with John Wallace on July 26, I want to make sure the record is clear. I provided you with a series of "real life hypo- thetical questions" for consideration by the staff in its memorandum to the Commissioners regarding advice the staff is giving on certain questions discussed at last month's Commission meeting. It was my understand- ing from the discussion at the "pre-agenda" meeting on July 23 that such questions were appropriate for sub- mission to the staff for discussion at the next Commission meeting. On July 26, John Wallace phoned me to say that the staff did not intend to address my questions in the memorandum for the upcoming Commission meeting. The staff intended to focus the memorandum in another area. Instead the staff wanted to treat my letter as one for "informal assistance." Mr. Wallace was seeking my concurrence in that transformation. I explained to Mr. Wallace that I was reluctant to agree, since an "informal assistance" response might be months in arriving. The questions posed in my letter are ones which must be dealt with before the November election cycle begins. Mr. Wallace gave me his personal assurance that my letter would be responded to within 21 working days, as if it were a "formal advice" letter. He pointed out that it would have an "I" number rather than an "A" number. With that assurance, I agreed to my letter being treated as one seeking "informal assistance."
